name: Jaraba - Calmarza Loop via GR 24
name_original: Santuario de Jaraba y Cascada Pozo Redondo de Calmarza por el Barranco de la Hoz Seca desde Ibdes
description: Tackle the difficult 19.4-mile touring cycling route from Ibdes, exploring dramatic canyons, waterfalls, and the Sanctuary of Jaraba.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Ibdes, Spain, traverse a landscape shaped by the Mesa River, featuring diverse natural formations and historical sites. The region is characterized by river valleys, deep ravines, and fertile lands, providing varied terrain for cyclists. This area offers a mix of routes that explore natural parks, waterfalls, and cultural landmarks. Best touring cycling routes around Ibdes The most popular touring cycling route is Monasterio de Piedra and Castillo de, a 14.6 miles (23.5 km) trail that takes 1 hour 50 minutes to complete. This route connects the Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park with a historical castle. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Jaraba - Calmarza Loop via GR 24, a difficult 19.4 miles (31.2 km) path. This loop follows a long-distance trail, offering varied scenery and elevation changes. Local touring cyclists also love the La Caprichosa Waterfall of Ibdes loop from Alhama de Aragón, a 40.6 miles (65.3 km) trail leading through areas featuring the La Caprichosa waterfall, often completed in about 4 hours 15 minutes. Touring cycling around Ibdes is defined by river valleys, deep ravines, and natural parks with waterfalls. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate to difficult routes. The routes in Ibdes are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from 10 reviews. More than 60 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Ibdes's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Ibdes?
A: There are 13 touring cycling routes around Ibdes listed on komoot, offering a variety of experiences for cyclists. These routes range from moderate to difficult, with options exploring river valleys, natural parks, and historical sites.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Ibdes?
A: The terrain around Ibdes is diverse, shaped by the Mesa River. You can expect routes that traverse river valleys, deep ravines, and fertile lands. Many routes also lead through natural parks and areas featuring impressive waterfalls, offering varied and scenic backdrops for your ride.

Q: Are there any easy touring cycling routes suitable for beginners in Ibdes?
A: While most routes around Ibdes are rated as difficult, there is one easy touring cycling route available. This route provides a gentler introduction to the region's landscapes for those new to touring cycling or looking for a more relaxed ride.

Q: Can I find routes that pass by waterfalls in the Ibdes area?
A: Yes, the Ibdes region is known for its beautiful waterfalls. You can explore routes that lead to natural wonders like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247403" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Caprichosa Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5335493" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Piedra Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5119827" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Chorreaderos Waterfall&lt;/a&gt;. One such route is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31497400" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Caprichosa Waterfall of Ibdes loop from Alhama de Aragón&lt;/a&gt;, which is a challenging 65.3 km ride.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes around Ibdes?
A: Yes, several circular touring cycling routes are available. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993043187/jaraba-calmarza-loop-via-gr-24" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jaraba - Calmarza Loop via GR 24&lt;/a&gt; offers a challenging 31.2 km ride, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31496964" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monterde Castle – Llumes loop from Nuévalos&lt;/a&gt; is another difficult 29.4 km option.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Ibdes is rich in historical and cultural sites. You can cycle past the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5247570" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of the Virgin of Jaraba&lt;/a&gt;, an 18th-century religious building partially embedded in rock. Routes also connect to areas near the Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park, which features a restored 13th-century Cistercian monastery. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993372931/monasterio-de-piedra-and-castillo-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Piedra and Castillo de&lt;/a&gt; route specifically links to a historical castle.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Ibdes?
A: The touring cycling routes in Ibdes are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from 10 reviews. Over 60 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore the area, often praising the varied terrain and the natural beauty of the river valleys and waterfalls.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park?
A: Yes, the Monasterio de Piedra Natural Park is a significant feature of the region. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993372931/monasterio-de-piedra-and-castillo-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Piedra and Castillo de&lt;/a&gt; route is a moderate 23.5 km ride that connects to the natural park, allowing you to experience its unique landscapes and attractions.

Q: What are some scenic viewpoints or natural features to look out for?
A: Beyond the waterfalls, the region offers several scenic spots. You can find highlights like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5119833" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mirror Lake (Lago del Espejo)&lt;/a&gt; and various viewpoints overlooking the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3287396" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de la Tranquera&lt;/a&gt;. The Mesa River itself carves out picturesque ravines, providing dynamic and changing vistas throughout your ride.

Q: What is the typical duration for a touring cycling route in Ibdes?
A: Route durations vary depending on distance and difficulty. For example, a moderate route like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e993372931/monasterio-de-piedra-and-castillo-de" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monasterio de Piedra and Castillo de&lt;/a&gt; typically takes around 1 hour 50 minutes, while a longer, difficult route such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31497400" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Caprichosa Waterfall of Ibdes loop from Alhama de Aragón&lt;/a&gt; can take about 4 hours 15 minutes.

Q: Are there any routes that start from or pass through Monasterio de Piedra?
A: Yes, there is a route that incorporates the Monasterio de Piedra area. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31496944" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Ibdes – La Caprichosa Waterfall of Ibdes loop from Monasterio de Piedra&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 43.0 km route that offers a journey through this scenic part of the region.

The Sanctuary of the Virgin of Jaraba is a place of great historical and spiritual interest, located in the stunning setting of the Barranco de la Hoz Seca. Built in the 18th century, the sanctuary is known for its architecture and natural setting.
It is said that the Virgin of Jaraba appeared on a rock oozing oil, giving it a miraculous quality.
We are next to the castle of Calmarza, where we can see a stately tower whose origin is unknown. It is 12 meters tall.
An essential corner for nature lovers. The "Pozo Redondo" Waterfall of Calmarza is a charming place that is hidden in the lower part of the town where the Mesa River passes

faqs.question: What type of bicycle is recommended for this route?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily designed for touring bicycles, but due to sections like the Barranco de la Hoz Seca, it is more suitable for mountain bikes or hybrid touring setups rather than traditional road bikes. The terrain can be rugged and challenging in parts.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route offers diverse terrain. Sections of the GR 24, particularly between Jaraba and Ibdes, are generally of low difficulty and suitable for cycling. However, the GR 24.1 through the Barranco de la Hoz Seca features more challenging and rugged terrain, described as a canyon single-track with limestone features, flowing sections, and small natural rock gardens. Expect many treacherous rocks and a need for careful navigation.

faqs.question: Are there any particularly challenging sections on the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Barranco de la Hoz Seca section is considered intermediate to difficult for mountain biking. It includes a notable obstacle: a 2-meter high vertical rock step with iron footstools that requires carrying your bike, especially if you have an e-bike. This section is a deep gorge with large rock walls, and while generally a pedestrian path, some parts might be cyclable with appropriate equipment and skill.

faqs.question: What natural and historical sights can I see along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter spectacular limestone canyons, including the Cañón del Río Mesa with walls over 100 meters high. Highlights include the La Caprichosa Waterfall of Ibdes, the peculiar Sanctuary of the Virgin of Jaraba, and the Calmarza Tower. The Barranco de la Hoz Seca also features ancient livestock corrals, old lime kilns, and 7,000-year-old cave paintings at Roca Benedí. Don't miss the Pozo Redondo Waterfall.

faqs.question: Can I see wildlife on this route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the area is known for its diverse wildlife. You have a good chance of spotting a colony of griffon vultures, especially from viewpoints like the Mirador de los Buitres near Calmarza. The natural landscape also supports various flora, including rosemary, lavender, gorse, savin juniper, and rose hips.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling route?
faqs.answer: The route starts from Ibdes. While specific parking details aren't provided, you should look for designated parking areas within Ibdes or near the trailhead. As this is part of the 'Senderos Turísticos de Aragón' network, there may be signage indicating suitable starting points and parking for cyclists.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Information on specific public transport options for cyclists to Ibdes, Jaraba, or Calmarza is not readily available. It is advisable to check local bus schedules or regional transport services for routes that accommodate bicycles, especially if you plan to use public transport for part of your journey.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees for this route?
faqs.answer: There is no mention of specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling this route or accessing the natural areas like the Barranco de la Hoz Seca. However, as parts of the route are within the Red Natura 2000 protected area, always respect local regulations and signage.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the potential for rockfalls and the rugged terrain, it's best to cycle this route during dry seasons when the weather is stable. Spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures and less risk of extreme conditions. Summer can be very hot, and winter might bring challenging conditions, especially in the canyons.

faqs.question: Is this cycling route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the route traverses natural areas, specific regulations regarding dogs on this cycling trail are not detailed. Generally, dogs are allowed on trails in natural parks, but it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially in protected areas like Red Natura 2000, to protect wildlife and ensure their safety on challenging terrain.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route primarily follows sections of the GR 24 and GR 24.1 long-distance trails. It also includes parts of the Jaraba-Calmarza, Ibdes-Jaraba, and Barranco de la Hoz Seca paths, among others.
